Throughout every period of the church, there have been some individuals more devoted to God, more consistent in their attendance upon his ordinances, and more willing to give up even lawful things for his sake than others. Some women, devoted to God and zealous for the tabernacle worship, expressed zeal by giving up their mirrors, which were polished plates of brass. Before looking-glasses were invented, these served the same purposes.

The walls of the court being only curtains indicated that the state of the Jewish church itself was movable and changeable; and in due time to be taken down and folded up, when the place of the tent should be enlarged, and its cords lengthened, to make room for the Gentile world.

The foundation of substantial pieces of silver showed the solidity and purity of the truth upon which the church is founded. Let us regard the Lord Jesus Christ while reading about the furniture of the tabernacle. While looking at the altar of burnt-offering, let us see Jesus. In him, his righteousness, and salvation, is a full and sufficient offering for sin. In the laver of regeneration, by his Holy Spirit, let our souls be washed, and they shall be clean; and as the people offered willingly, so may our souls be made willing. Let us be ready to give up anything, and count all but loss to win Christ.
